coredata.dc:title: “Enhancing electrochemical performance of Fe@Ir/GQDs electrodes with MoS₂ for advanced energy storage and hydrogen evolution Reaction”
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zaka, Asma
Waqas Iqbal, Muhammad
Mohsen, Q.
Amin, Mohammed A.
Alrobei, Hussein
coredata.dc:description: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have unique properties that make them important in energy storage systems. The layered structure, edge locations, wide surface area, and closeness impact of MoS2/GQDs nanostructures enhance their ability for energy storage. We are effectively utilizing the hydrothermal technique to synthesize Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs, a new composite electrode material for supercapattery energy storage devices. Using a three-electrode setup, we are assessing the electrochemical performance of Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs. Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs is showing exceptional electrochemical qualities, demonstrating an excellent specific capacity of 1104C/g in an electrolyte solution containing 1 M KOH. The Ir@Fe-MOF/MoS2/GQDs is exhibiting energy and power density of around 53 W h kg−1 and 2652 W kg−1, respectively. A Coulombic efficiency of 92.23 % and 96.14 % of capacity are being maintained by the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC material after 5000 cycles of alternative GCD measurements. The results of this work are showing that supercapattery applications can benefit from the new electrode material Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs. The HER is having a lower potential barrier of 32.12 mV dec-1 with a 130 mV overpotential at −10 mA cm−2 due to the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs composite. This work is providing a way to develop effective bimetallic MOFs nanocomposite materials for use in biomedical and future energy storage systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Iron-MOF
Iridium-MOF
MoS2
Graphene quantum dots
Cyclic voltammetry
Galvanostatic charge/discharge
Specific capacity
Real device
Energy density
And power density

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) An illustration showing all steps involved in synthesizing Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2@GQDs and evaluating its hydrothermal electrochemical measurements (b) Synthesized method of GQDs.

(a) XRD pattern of Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs (b) SEM image of composite material Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs (c) Raman spectroscopy of Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs.
(a) XPS spectra of Fe 2p (b) Ir 4f (c) Mo 3d (d) O ls (e) S 2p (f) C ls.
(a) BET analysis spectrum of Fe@Ir-MOF, (b) Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 (c) Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs nanocomposite (d) EIS spectrum of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs.

(a) Fe@Ir-MOF CV measurements at 3 to 50 mV/s scan rates (b) Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 CV measurements at 3 to 50 mV/s (c) Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs CV measurements at 3 to 50 mV/s scan rates (d) Comparison of specific capacities through CV Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs nanocomposite.

(a) Fe@Ir-MOF GCD measurements at various current densities 1 to 2.6 A/g (b) Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 GCD measurements at various current densities 1 to 2.6 A/g (c) Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs GCD measurements at various current densities 1 to 2.6 A/g (d) Comparison of specific capacities through GCD Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs at different scan rates.

(a) Systematic illustration of two electrode assembly for supercapattery (b) CV measurements of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC at different scan rates (c) b-fitting value graph of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC (d) GCD measurements of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC at different current densities.

(a) Specific capacity of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC through CV measurements at different scan rates (b) (a) Specific capacity of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC through GCD measurements at different current densities (c) 5000 no. of cycles for Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC (c) capacity retention of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC.
(a) Energy and power density of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC.

(a) Linear sweep Voltammetry for Fe@Ir-MOF, Pt/C, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs(b) Overpotential graph for Fe@Ir-MOF, Pt/C, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs(c) Tafel slope for Fe@Ir-MOF, Pt/C, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs (d) L.S.V. of Fe@Ir-MOF and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs before and after 1000 cycles (e) EIS spectrum of Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs (f) Chronopotentiometry curves of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs and Pt/C catalysts at potential of − 10 mA cm-2.
Comparison of stability test for Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC device with literature.
Showed comparison of energy and power densities for Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs.

head.ce:title: “Enhancing electrochemical performance of Fe@Ir/GQDs electrodes with MoS₂ for advanced energy storage and hydrogen evolution Reaction”

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Riphah International University, Campus Lahore, Pakistan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Riphah International University, Campus Lahore, Pakistan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, College of Engineering, Prince Sattam Bin Abdul Aziz University, Al-Kharj 11942, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have unique properties that make them important in energy storage systems. The layered structure, edge locations, wide surface area, and closeness impact of MoS2/GQDs nanostructures enhance their ability for energy storage. We are effectively utilizing the hydrothermal technique to synthesize Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs, a new composite electrode material for supercapattery energy storage devices. Using a three-electrode setup, we are assessing the electrochemical performance of Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs. Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs is showing exceptional electrochemical qualities, demonstrating an excellent specific capacity of 1104C/g in an electrolyte solution containing 1 M KOH. The Ir@Fe-MOF/MoS2/GQDs is exhibiting energy and power density of around 53 W h kg−1 and 2652 W kg−1, respectively. A Coulombic efficiency of 92.23 % and 96.14 % of capacity are being maintained by the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC material after 5000 cycles of alternative GCD measurements. The results of this work are showing that supercapattery applications can benefit from the new electrode material Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs. The HER is having a lower potential barrier of 32.12 mV dec-1 with a 130 mV overpotential at −10 mA cm−2 due to the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs composite. This work is providing a way to develop effective bimetallic MOFs nanocomposite materials for use in biomedical and future energy storage systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In order to address global warming and the depletion of conventional fossil fuels, clean, green energy sources are becoming increasingly popular [1,2]. Supercapacitors (SCs), among other energy storage methods [3–5], have attracted much attention in promoting green energy storage technology because of their remarkable power density, quick recharge time, remarkable cycle stability, and superior shielding. [6–8]. Both forms of SCs that are commonly recognized are electrochemical double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) as well as pseudocapacitors (PCs) [9,10]. Supercapacitors have been used instead of batteries that require capacitors because they can produce better power and energy densities. The electrode's material, which ought to have a porous nature and be highly conductive, determines the device's overall performance. A great deal of research has been performed to develop novel technologies for storing energy. Supercapattery, a novel concept combining supercapacitors' advantages with batteries, is becoming increasingly popular. The binary nanocomposites consist of two distinct nanomaterials that complement each other effectively to optimize their respective benefits. conquer the inadequacies of the individual components is the aim of this combination. Combining metal sulphides or oxides with carbon-based materials like graphene or reduced graphene oxides (rGO) is one way to increase both conductivity and capacitance at the same time. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which are essentially graphene nanoparticles with sizes between 2 and 50 nm, have garnered a lot of interest because of their special properties. They consist of strong conductivity, enhanced surface area, and very high solubility in a variety of solvents. Consequently, GQDs have broad applications in a variety of fields, such as health, energy conversion, as well as storage [11]. GQDs have an entirely novel type of carbon-based illuminating nanomaterials [12]. GQDs above semiconductor quantum dots due to their superior photostability, chemical inertness, reduced hazardous effects, high biological compatibility, along with unique optoelectronic properties [13,14]. Natural GQDs have better surface area as well as interval-to-diameter ratios, among other physical and chemical characteristics [15]. By combining the benefits of three different components, ternary nanocomposites provide a multimodal solution to energy storage device problems, resulting in improved ultimate efficiency [16–18].

Mumtaz et al.devised a composite electrode based on Gr to observe the electrochemical characteristics. The ZnNbS@Gr composite electrode's sample remarkable specific capacity value of 1591.76 Cg−1 was caused by improvements in the redox-active sites and charge conversion [19]. The potential electrocatalytic water splitting method can produce hydrogen, which can be used to generate a clean, carbon-free fuel. Hydrogen generated through the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER; 2H+ + 2e_- H2) holds great potential for a variety of commercial, industrial, as well as transportation uses. Improving the electrocatalytic water splitting process's productivity, efficiency, and affordability is essential to determining hydrogen as a viable and appealing source of energy in the near future [20].

The class of transition metal compounds having a metallic structure includes iridium MOF (Ir-MOF), which exhibits metal-like conductivity. It has been applied in the industry for electrical, magnetic, and electrochemical applications because of its metal-like conductivity [21,22]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are multidimensional and can be modified to specific needs. [23,24]. Severalmetal-centered MOFs have been studied regardingpotential energy storage uses. The basic elements of MOFs are metal nodes and organic ligands, which have been thoroughly investigated for their possible use in energy storage. [25–27]. Their framework, which is both structurally complex and permeable, can be modified to exhibit different features by adjusting the ratios of organic ligands and metal nodes [28,29]. Supercapacitor performance is also significantly improved by the abundance of redox-active sites offered by MOFs' porous structure. Together with other functional materials, MOFs significantly improve conductivity and electrochemical characteristics. [30,31]. Recently, bimetallic MOFs have been directly carbonized. This has produced synthetic diverse Ir as well as MgO contained in nonporous carbon framework [32]. In several studies, Iron-MOFs, and Fe-MOFs are useful as supercapacitors with defined coordination chemistry and a large, rich redox content. One common and abundant metal is iron (Fe) [33]. Organic ligands strongly alter Fe ion electrochemistry, including redox potential and valence states. Many studies focus on organic ligands since they are important in MOF electrochemistry. As an organic linker, 4,4′-bipyridine improves MOFs. [19]. 4,4′-Bipyridine provides a 3D porous framework for hybrid device electrochemical operations by linking MOF metal sites.

Irsignificant pseudocapacitance is one of its unique characteristics [34]. However, high-surface-area iridium dioxide is seldom investigated in electrochemical capacitor applications in combination with carbonaceous materials. Because organic ligands are essential to MOFs' electrochemical reactions, several studies have given them attention. Benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylate or 1,4-benzene dicarboxylate (BDC) moieties are employed as an organic linker for MOFs with advanced-level development [35,36]. In order to create a three-dimensional porous structure Essential for efficient electrochemical responses in hybrid devices, BDC connects the metal nodes in MOFs [37]. The numerous active sites and porosity of the bimetallic MOFs are linked with the electrodes' remarkable power and energy density in SCs. These characteristics increase the capacity to store charges and the availability of charge transporters. [38].

Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) is considered the most economical transition metal chalcogenide used as an electrode in batteries and supercapacitors. Due to this layered MoS2 nanostructures, the electrode performs well with a large surface area that is intrinsic, with edge locations [39]. The main characteristics that stand out, such as the large surface area and low cost of solution-based hydrothermal synthesis, increase the potential uses of MoS2 nanostructures in energy storage devices. The durable ionic conductivity and capacity of MoS2 nanosheets have attracted the attention of scientists, resulting in the standard for transition metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) [40,41]. MoS2 is a multilayered semiconductor having a small band gap of 1.8 eV [42]. The remarkably high current densities, the multilayer hybrid metallic sulfide@MoS2 composite exhibits excellent rate capability and cycle stability [43].

Additionally, the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 composite electrode demonstrated enhanced lasting stability and a decreased over potential of 129 mV, making it a viable electrode for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) activities [44,45]. With a specific capacity of 1104C/g, the Fe@Ir-MOF/ MoS2/GQDs nanocomposite exhibits remarkable electrochemical efficiency. A hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) was constructed using a Fe@Ir-MOF/GQDs composite for the positive electrode in order to examine the electrochemical characteristics of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs nanocomposite in more detail [46]. The key advantages of ternary nanocomposites are their high electrical conductivity and many redox reactions, which are essential for developing effective energy storage devices [47,48].

This study applies the enhanced Hummers method to obtain the GQDs. The Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 nanocomposite has been synthesized into hybrid supercapacitorshydrothermally. First, morphological, structural, and elemental composition properties are studied. Its morphology, crystallinity, analysis of elements, and material homogeneity are investigated using SEM, XRD, XPS, and BET. In order to determine the ideal settings for the device's efficiency, the influence of the electrolyte's temp is also investigated. The designed asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) with the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC showed exceptional power and energy density at a potential window of 1.6 eV. This research gives a basis for using bimetallic MOFto improve energy storage devices' efficiency.

N, N'-dimethylformamide (DMF), hydrofluoric acid (HF), ethanol, benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic (C9H6O6), 1,4-benzene-dicarboxylate moieties, benzene 4,4′-Bipyridine polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVDF), and iron(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.
Fe@Ir-MOF was made using deionized water, iron(III) nitrates nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O), terephthalic acid (H2BDC), as well as iridium(IV) hexahydrate (Cl6H14IrO6) for the solvent used.

The molar concentrations of iron(III) nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) and iridium(IV) hexahydrate (Cl6H14IrO6) needed to generate the final Fe@Ir-MOF product having a suitable Fe: Ir ratio were identified accurately. Equal molar amounts of IrO6 (1 M) and Fe(NO3)3 (1 M) were used. The metal nitrates were mixed in deionized water (DIW) to ensure complete dissolution. To achieve the required stoichiometry, a unique H2BDC mixture was made in deionized water (DI water) with a constant H2BDC molecular ratio in the entire molar ratio of metal nitrates. Then H2BDC mixture was continuously added to the metal nitrates mixture while constantly stirring. After stirring for an hour, the resulting mixture was moved to an autoclave set to operate at a high temperature of 120 °C in a hydrothermal method [49,50]. After the process, the impurities were extracted by centrifuging the produced Fe@Ir-MOF and cleaning it with DIW. Afterward, an oven was used to produce Fe@Ir −MOF from the collected material. Fig. 1 a shows the preparation of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs using the hydrothermal method.

GQDs were produced according to an earlier report. [51].To summarize, 2.0 g of citric acid was subjected to heating at 160 ◦C for 30 min until an orange liquid formed. The pH of the orange liquid was then adjusted to 9 carefully and dropwise, adding 100.0 ml of sodium hydroxide solution and 10.0 mg per liter. According to previous studies, the highest yield and size were reached at this pH [52,53] and simultaneously agitated the solution. Lastly, for subsequent usage, the synthesized GQDs were stored in a refrigerator at 40◦C. Fig. 1b shows the synthesis process of GQDs.

The Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2@GQDs nanocomposite material was made by directly combining GQDs with MoS2. Using a tip sonication technique, the MoS2 nano-power was extracted by the two-dimensional material and then sonicated to disperse the bulk MoS2. Fe@Ir-MOF/ MoS2 with GQDs were combined, and the optimal ratio was 10 % GQDs to 90 % Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 [54–56]. The mass-to-weight ratios of Fe@Ir-MOF (50 %) MoS2 (40 %) and GQDs (10 %) were used in the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs composite.

The electrode designed using nickel foam. The surface of the Ni-foam was cleaned to get rid of contaminants and native oxides in order to obtain the most economical price for particular capacity. Hydrogen chloride, deionizedwater, and ethanol were used to achieve this cleansing. After utilizing the PVDF to create a homogenous mixture, acetylene black added to an NMP solvent in percentages of 75 %, 10 %, and 15 %, accordingly, as well as applied to the Ni-foam surface. The composite materialwas stirred for 7.5 h [57,58]. The resultant slurry applied to 1 x 1 cm2 of NF substrate. After the composite was ready, it was placed on the NF and dried for five hours at 75 °C. In addition, the Activated carbonelectrode served asa negative electrodewhile Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs servedasa positive electrode for real device measurements. The CV curve's rectangular shape, which also shows that the deposition was effective, confirms that AC is capacitive. Equation (1) is used to calculate the specific capacity from CV [59].
(1)
Qs =
1
2 v m
∫
vf
vi
I × v d v
The Qs (C/g), the area under the curve, is measured by ∫ vf vi (I×V)dV. “V” stands for the voltage, “m” for the mass of the material, and “v” for the scan rate. Equation (2) is used to calculate the specific capacity using GCD [60,61],
(2)
Qs =
I × Δ T
m
Δt stands for the discharge time in seconds (s), m for the active mass in grams, and I for the standardized specific current in amperes. The formulae were used to calculate the energy and power densities [Ed and Pd] [60],
(3)
E d
=
Q × Δ V
2 × 3.6
(4)
P d
=
E × 3600
Δ T
Ed and Pd are accordingly measured using equations (3) and (4). In this case, the “ V ” indicates the potential in the GCD computations. In equation (4), ''t' represents the discharge time value.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) is used to investigate the crystal structure of Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs nanocomposites. XRD was used to analyze the nanocomposite materials' phase purity and crystalline structure as shown in Fig. 2 a. The Fe@Ir-MOF XRD patterns demonstrate the material's crystallinity and distinct peak intensities, and the fact that no extra diffraction planes are present supports the material's exceptional phase purity. The Fe-MOF cubic arrangement's phase identity was confirmed by the diffraction peaks at 29.37 and 34.25, which had lattice points (220) and (310), respectively [62]. Furthermore, the diffraction pattern of the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2GQDs nanocomposite is displayed in Fig. 2a. MoS2′s hexagonal phase is shown the peaks in the pattern at 2θ 15.03◦ (002), 27.5◦ (100), 40.02◦ (103), and 63.04◦ (201) [63]. The presence of GQDs is confirmed by the peak in the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs nanocomposite, which appears at 34.25◦ and is consistent with the (301) plane [54].

Fig. 2b shown the image of the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs materials through scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The nanoflake structure of the MoS2/GQDs materials in Fe@Ir-MOF, where the flakes' few-layer thickness and transparency imply the existence of GQDs nanosheets that achieved excellent interaction with MoS2 nanosheets, confirms the presence of dopants [54]. In order to retain an extensive amount of charge carriers and maintain their transparency, the MoS2 and GQD nanoflakes increased the surface area and pores of the surface.

An additional analytical method called Raman spectroscopy can reveal further details about the synthesized nanocomposites' chemical composition and vibrational modes Fig. 2c. The presence of the organic linkers 1,4-benzenedicarboxylate and benzene 4,4′-Bipyridine in the Fe@Ir-MOF structure is indicated by the peaks at 213 cm−1, 303 cm−1, and 401 cm−1, which match the Fe@Ir-MOF nanoparticles may be present, as suggested by the vibrational modes while the Co metal D band is shown by the peaks at 500, 700 and 938 cm−1. The spectral peaks detected at 380 and 404 cm−1 were attributed to carbon bending modes and vibrational stretching of E2g and A1g bonds. As a result, the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 composite's single pattern displayed all the modes of the separate Fe@Ir-MOF and MoS2, proving that the mixed composite had formed successfully. The Raman signaturesof the distinct parts of the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs composite align with the peaks noticed in the composite, suggesting that the components have effectively integrated into it [64,65]. Alterations in peak positions in the Raman spectrum indicated shifts in the material's chemical composition and modifications. The bonding environment and molecular structure are impacted by interactions between the Fe@Ir-MOF and MoS2 components, where those modifications occur. Furthermore, the Raman spectrum provides valuable insights into the composite material degree of instability and crystal quality. The width and intensity of the peaks indicate the size of the crystalline domains and the material degree of crystallinity.

The Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs nanocomposite's elemental composition is investigated using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS). The high-resolution spectra of the Fe 2p area are shown in Fig. 3 a. 19.6 eV for spin-energy gap, the detected peaks at 1025.7 eV and 1045.3 eV correspond to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. Moreover, two spin–orbit doublet patterns can be seen in the high-resolution Ir 4f spectra for Ir Fig. 3b. Ir 4f7/2 and Ir 4f5/2 are the identities of the peaks that pair at 62.1 and 65.1 eV, respectively. According to Fig. 3c the pure Mo 3d high-resolution XPS spectra may be deconvolved into 3peaks, which are Mo4+ 3d5/2 (232.21 eV), Mo4+ 3d3/2 (234.55 eV), and Mo6+ 3d5/2 (235.41 eV), accordingly [56]. With a major peak at 284.4 eV, the most common XPS spectra for GQDs are shifted in Fig. 3d [54,66].

The electrode's efficiency may be raised by modifying the pore size, pore volume, as well as surface area all of all material can be computed using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET). Furthermore, the precise surface areas of the samples are determined using the BET nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm Fig. 4 (a-c). Fe@Ir-MOF has pore volumes of around 13 cm3g−1, typical pore sizes of 20.17 nm, and surface areas of 26.25 m2g−1. The values for Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 are about pore volumes of around 17 cm3g−1, typical pore sizes of 24.32 nm, and surface areas of 31.12 m2g−1 and the values for Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs are about pore volumes of around 23.09 cm3g−1, typical pore sizes of 31.19 nm, and surface areas of 52.18 m2g−1. Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs with a higher surface area per unit volume have more accessible sites that react during adsorption, maintaining photocatalytic activity [67]. The findings of the BET study show that Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD's greater surface area permits a more notable buildup of charges on the electrode surface.

According to the Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) results, the doped material (Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDS) improved conductivity [68,69], which is crucial for supercapacitors in Fig. 4d. The Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD electrode displays a more significant EIS slope when compared to the other electrodes (Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDS. Components like the structure of the electrolyte and equivalent series resistance (ESR) could be responsible for this disparity. The ESR values for Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD are 1.601, 1.314, and 1.012, respectively [70].

Fig. 5 (a-c) displays the cyclic voltammetry (CV) findings for the Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD composites. A range of scan speeds, from 3 to 50 mV/s, was used with the potential window remaining constant at 0.75 V. The electrochemical behaviour of the active materials is exhibited by the ensuing CV profiles. The significantly greater specific capacity of the composite material validates Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD's enhanced performance. The CV curves of all synthetic materials exhibit distinct redox properties, demonstrating pseudocapacitive behaviour and confirming the presence of a redox process.

The remarkable constancy of the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD composite material's CV graphs, even at a higher scan speed of 50 mV/s, makes them significant. When compared to comparable synthesized materials, this demonstrates the material's exceptional rate capability [71–74]. This suggests that there could be limits on the device's reversibility or the rate at which charge can be stored. If it is feasible to distinguish between the reduction and oxidation peaks on the parallel side, an irreversible process is suggested. This implies that redox reactions result in significant losses and that charge storage is delayed. When examining electrode materials for energy storage, identifying out if the electrochemical process is reversible or irreversible is essential. This information aids in evaluating the suitability and effectiveness of the objects. [75]. The Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD exhibit capacitive behavior, preventing the material reduction peak often found in publications. [76,77]. Their participation in the crystallization process of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD was impeded by the quantity of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD nanoparticles. These nanoparticles can therefore combine to form channel that allow current to flow [78,79]. Fe@Ir-MOF increases conductivity in Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 via improving electron and ion mobility through electrostatic absorption/desorption. Electrically charged particles in the material move faster and areuninhibited by this phenomenon. [80]. The Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD composites also showed CV curves with peak currents, indicating improved the performance of charge storage, increase the conductivity as well as increase the mobility of electrons due to doping the MoS2 and GQDs. Charge accumulation by the material determines the extent of these CV curves. [70]. Thus, as shown in Fig. 5, the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 composite has a significantly greater area than Fe@Ir-MOF alone. Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 exhibits more capacity and electroactive characteristics than Fe@Ir-MOF, as seen by the broader part of the graph when analyzed separately. Since the CV curves do not significantly shift, higher scan rates suggest that the synthesized materials are durable. This consistency demonstrates the electrodes' ability to charge and discharge quickly, showing their durability and effectiveness for high-performance energy storage applications[81]. Specific capacity and overall performance decrease at faster scan rates due to less interaction caused by the electrolyte ions' shorter reaction time with the electrode. The efficiency of the energy storage device may eventually decrease as a result of this reduced contact time leading to increased kinetic irreversibility [82]. These graphs show an improvement in the electrochemical characteristics and show the combined effect of the composite materials. Pristine Fe-MOF having a specific capacity of 411C/g at a scan speed of 3 mV/s. Under the same circumstances, the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 composite has a higher specific capacity of 734C/g. These composite material Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD exhibited the highest specific capacity of all the samples; it reached 1104C/g at the same 3 mV/s scan rate Fig. 5d. Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD composites are highly electroactive and have a large surface area, provide additional active sites for effective charge storage. Fig. 5d compares the specific capacity of all the materials through CV.

Using the galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD) technique, the charge storage capacity of the Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD electrodes was assessed. A GCD study representing the electrochemical characteristics of the Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD is presented in Fig. 6 (a-c). After examination, the Fe@Ir-MOF electrode was found to have an unusually low specific capacity [83]. Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD composites were used to test GCD. Current densities varied from 1 to 2.6 A/g at 0.65 V. Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 GCD curves are displayed in Fig. 6b and 6c. The findings demonstrated that as current density increased, correspondingly increased the length of the discharging phase. When current densities were changed, the Fe@Ir-MOF GCD curves showed the expected typical characteristics. The discharge peaks of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD composites are greater than those of Fe@Ir-MOF. Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD combined having a greater effect on electrical charge storage and discharge peak. Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD enhances the discharge peak via improving charge conduction. The GCD findings confirm the CV graph and demonstrate the battery-like characteristics of the Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD electrodes. These figures illustrate the cumulative effect of the composite materials and clearly indicate an improvement in the electrochemical characteristics. Pure Fe-MOF has an exact capacity of 633C/g at 1 A/g. Under comparable circumstances, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 has a higher specific capacity of 1131C/g. With a specific capacity of 1701C/g at 1 A/g, the composite material Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD has the highest value in Fig. 6d. The Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD composites have a higher surface area and exceptional electroactivity, which results in more active sites for efficient charge storage. CV is used in Fig. 6d to compare each material's particular capacity. The GCD results indicate that Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD has a lot of promise for use in supercapattery systems and confirm previous CV results.

Asymmetric two-electrode analysis was carried out using the electrode doped with Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD. The supercapattery two-electrode assembly design is depicted in Fig. 7 a. A comparison was made between the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD positive electrode and the activated carbon negative electrode. The Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD composite was attached to a nickel foam current collector, and the activated carbon cathode was maintained apart from a porous membrane [84]. Activated carbon (AC) was specifically chosen to function as the device's negative electrode due to its high porosity and conductivity. The improved ion absorption from the electrolyte is made possible by this unique combination [85]. By using the various scan rates lies between 3 mV/s to 100 mV/S, Fig. 7b shown the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD electrochemical efficiency. The CV peaks of the supercapattery device showed quasi-rectangular patterns; however, when the potentials rose, the resultant forms were distorted. The limited mobility of ions through the redox processes at electrode surface is the reason for the non-ideal form of the cyclic voltammogram curves, particularly at higher scan rates. This limit limits active sites from being used to their full capacity, leading to forms other than rectangles. A hybrid device contains a set b-value between 0.5 with 0.8 as well as combines the benefits of batteries plus supercapacitors [86]. The device's unique characteristics and dependability are highlighted by its b-values, which are displayed in Fig. 7c and support the classification of this device as a supercapattery. As demonstrated in Fig. 7c, the b-values of the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD//AC supercapattery device suggest that it may be classified as a supercapattery [87].

The cyclic voltammogram curves of the supercapattery device using Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD demonstrate its exceptional rate capabilities, with noticeably higher scan rates. The quasi-rectangular forms of the peaks in the CV became more distorted as the potentials increased. The device's two distinct energy storage systems, which cause various reactions, are responsible for this behaviour. The CV curves become non-rectangular at upper scan rates due to some limitations on ion transport through the electrode surface electrochemical oxidation–reduction process. This limitation reduces the use of the active site, leaving it inefficient. Fig. 7d showed GCD curves for the hybrid device constructed with Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD. These GCD curves have a distinctive form that resembles the humped as well as triangular shapes seen in the cyclic voltammetry investigation. The GCD curves for the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDS supercapattery device at different current densities are shown in Fig. 7d. The supercapattery device uses Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD to maintain the potential range between 0 and 1.6 V constant.

The apparent reliability of the GCD graphs at different current densities indicates that performance was constant. The lowest voltage drop across the internal resistance (IR) indicates a reduction in total resistance. This formula used to determine the issue's capacity is derived from the GCD graphs. [88].
(5)
C m
=
∫
V 1
V 2
I / d V
2 m v Δ V
(6)
C m
= I × Δ t / m
Equation (5),6) calculates the specific capacity by considering the mass of active material substrates (m), discharge current (I), and discharging duration (t). Fig. 8 a and Fig. 8b show the precise capacity obtained by CV and GCD of the real device, respectively. Utilising CV resultswith a 3 mV/s scan rate, the supercapattery device (Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD//AC) demonstrated a specific capacity of 167C/g. Additionally, the GCD investigation demonstrated that the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD/AC combination showed a specific capacity of 210C/g at a current density of 1 A/g. The capacity of an energy storage device decreases as the current density increases. The decrease in the contact between the ion and the electrode material is mostly caused by the short time.

Cycle life is an important factor to take into attention. It describes the ability of supercapattery devices to sustain performance across several charge–discharge cycles. The 5000 number of cycles for Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC are displayed in Fig. 8c. The highest columbic efficiency attained throughout these cycles is 88.23 %, as shown in Fig. 8d. Moreover, after 5000 cycles, 96.14 % of the capacity could be kept. The Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD/AC supercapattery device exhibited exceptional capacity storage and cycle stability.

Table 1 . Comparison of stability test for Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs//AC device with literature.Higher energy density means the device can store more energy. The following formulas calculated energy and power density.
(7)
E =
Q × Δ V
2 × 3.6
(8)
P =
E × 3600
Δ t
Variables like potential window (ΔV), specific capacity (Q), energy density (E), discharge duration (Δt), and power density (P) are used in the calculations.

The supercapattery device (Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD//AC) has a specific energy of 53 Wh/kg and a power of 2652 W/kg. The rates at which the device accumulates and releases energy are shown by this data. The comparison of the findings from this investigation and those from previous studies is presented in Fig. 9 . Table 2 . energy and power densities for Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs were compared and shown.

Using a standard three-electrode setup, the HER of Fe@Ir-MOF, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD nanocomposites were determined [89,90]. The efficacy of the coated electrodes was evaluated in the HER experiment against the most effective Pt/C reference electrode [91,92]. Out of all the composites, the electrode coated with Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD has the greatest HER catalytic activity. It is, however, less than the electrode decorated with Pt/C. This is demonstrated by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves [93,94] Fig. 10 a Pt/C, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD, Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2, and Fe@Ir-MOF overpotentials are summarized as 45.15, 71.23, 97.31, and 120.12 mV, accordingly, in Fig. 10b. This suggests that because the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD binary nanocomposite needs a much lower overpotential to achieve equiv-1, it functions better. In the early stages of the HER, an H+ attaches to the catalyst's surface, resulting in the current density [95,96]. The Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQDs composite exhibits a lower Tafel slope (59 mV dec-1) but lower than Pt/C (41 mV dec-1) compared to Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2 (82 mV dec-1) and Fe@Ir-MOF (99 mV dec-1) when hydrogen is adsorbed. Equation (9) suggests that this phase is called the discharge step or the Volmer step. Then, H* combines up with an H+ and an electron (e-) to produce an H2, as expressed in equation (10). Conversely, H2 can be produced by the Tafel step, also known as the chemical desorption step [97]. For this to occur, two H* atoms must align up on the catalyst's surface, as shown by equation (11). The whole response of the HER is displayed in equation (12), which also establishes the standard electrode potential (E1) and uses it to estimate the average electrode potential of electrochemical processes [98].
(9)
H
+
+
e
-
→
H
∗

H
∗
+
H
+
+
e
-
→
H
2
(11)
2
H
∗
→
H
2
(12)

H
∗
+ 2
e
-
→
H
2
Furthermore, Fig. 10c shows that the Pt/C has an ideal value in contrast to this research. An electrocatalyst's stability has to be determined; this may be achieved by measuring CV for 1000 cycles at 2 mV s−1. The sample's long-term sustainability is explained in Fig. 10d, where the LSV curve exhibits a little variation before and after 1000 cycles. The remarkable stability of the Fe@Ir-MOF nanocomposite can be attributed to its crystal phase stability and structural strength.

This study uses hydrothermal techniques to synthesize the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD nanocomposite electrode material. XRD, SEM, and XPS are used to analyze the electrode material's structural, compositional, and elemental composition. The electrochemical characteristics of Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD are investigated in relation to the temperature of the electrolytes. Additionally, the electrochemical characteristics of the real device were tested and designed effectively. The device demonstrated a surprising specific capacity of 210C/g at 1 A/g together with high energy and power densities of 53 Wh/Kg and 2652 W/Kg. In addition, the apparatus is tested for 5000 cycles, resulting in a coulomb efficiency of 92.23 % and a retention capacity of 96.16 % after 5000 GCD cycles. In addition, energy storage applications and electrochemical hydrogen evaluation reactions employ the Fe@Ir-MOF/MoS2/GQD nanocomposite electrode material.
Asma Zaka: Writing – original draft. Muhammad Waqas Iqbal: Supervision. Q. Mohsen: . Mohammed A. Amin: . Hussein Alrobei: .
